articles.title: UN Votes To Support Strong Action On Climate Change, Including Reparations
articles.content:
联合国（美联社）——联合国大会周三以压倒性多数投票支持限制气候变化的强有力行动，尽管美国最近进行了外交努力，试图撤回该决议。

拥有193个成员国的联合国大会批准了一项不具约束力的决议，支持联合国最高法院去年7月发布的里程碑式咨询意见。该意见称，各国未能保护地球免受气候变化危害构成违反国际法。

“国际法院已经发声。今天，大会作出了回应，”联合国秘书长安东尼奥·古特雷斯在一份声明中表示。“这是对国际法、气候正义、科学以及各国保护人民免受日益严峻的气候危机影响的责任的有力肯定。”

投票结果为141票赞成、8票反对、28票弃权。美国、俄罗斯、伊朗和沙特阿拉伯——一些最大的石油生产国和主要温室气体排放国——投票反对该决议。气候变化主要由燃烧煤炭、石油和天然气引起。

决议文本包括：采取国家气候行动计划，将全球气温升幅限制在1.5摄氏度（2.7华氏度）以下；逐步取消对化石燃料勘探、生产和开采的补贴；并敦促违约方提供“充分赔偿”。

2015年《巴黎气候协定》设定的目标是，将工业化前（19世纪中叶）以来的升温幅度限制在1.5摄氏度以内，由此产生了“1.5度以下才能生存”的口号。但现在科学家表示，即使在他们最乐观的情况下，仍将超过这一标志性温度线。

该联合国决议最初包含国际法院意见中更强有力的措辞，呼吁建立“国际损害登记册”以记录证据和索赔，但经过近十轮磋商后被删除，以争取更多支持。

该决议的通过正值美联社2月报道特朗普政府曾敦促其他 nations 推动小岛国瓦努阿图——该草案的原始发起国——撤回该决议。

美国国务院向所有美国驻外使领馆发布的指导意见称，它“强烈反对”该提案，并表示其通过“可能对美国工业构成重大威胁”。

周三，美国常驻联合国副大使塔米·布鲁斯再次猛烈抨击该决议，称其“高度有问题”，并坚称华盛顿对此有严重的法律和政策担忧，尽管草案已做修改。

“该决议包含关于化石燃料和其他气候议题的不当政治要求，”布鲁斯在投票前告诉大会。

但瓦努阿图和其他面临气候变化生存威胁的岛国代表表示，让大会支持法院意见很重要。该意见被誉为国际气候法的转折点。

“我们应该坦诚地告诉彼此为什么这很重要，”瓦努阿图常驻联合国大使奥多·特维在投票前表示。“它很重要，因为危害是真实的，而且已经到来——就在我们的岛屿和海岸线上，就在面临干旱和收成失败的社区。”

他补充说：“承受最沉重负担的国家和人民往往是对问题贡献最少的。”

这个世界机构的行动之前，太平洋国家经历了数十年的沮丧，眼睁睁看着家园消失。

在图瓦卢，平均海拔仅2米（6.6英尺），超过三分之一的人口已申请前往澳大利亚的气候移民签证，尽管每年获批人数有限。预计到2100年，该国大部分地区将在涨潮时被淹没。

在瑙鲁，政府已开始向富有的外国人出售护照——提供数十个国家的免签入境——以期为可能的搬迁工作筹集资金。

人权观察组织联合国主任路易·夏布朗诺周三在一份声明中表示，通过认可法院裁决，联合国“重申了保护人权的全球承诺”。他指出，这是“尽管美国和其他石油生产国试图扼杀应对气候变化的努力”。
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

UNITED NATIONS (AP) — The U.N. General Assembly voted overwhelmingly Wednesday to support strong action to limit climate change despite recent diplomatic efforts by the United States to have the measure withdrawn.

The 193-member world body approved a nonbinding resolution endorsing the landmark advisory opinion by the U.N.’s top court last July that called failure by countries to protect the planet from climate change a violation of international law.

Advertisement

“The world’s highest court has spoken. Today, the General Assembly has answered,” U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres said in a statement. “This is a powerful affirmation of international law, climate justice, science, and the responsibility of states to protect people from the escalating climate crisis.”

The vote was 141-8 with 28 abstentions. The U.S., Russia, Iran and Saudi Arabia — some of the highest oil-producing nations and major greenhouse gas emitters — opposed the measure. Climate change is caused mainly by the burning of coal, oil and gas.

The text includes adopting a national climate action plan to limit global temperature rise to below 1.5 degrees Celsius (2.7 degrees Fahrenheit); phasing out subsidies for fossil fuel exploration, production and exploitation; and urging those in violation to provide “full reparation” for damage.

Advertisement

The Paris climate agreement in 2015 set a goal of limiting warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius since pre-industrial times, or the mid-1800s, giving rise to the mantra “1.5 to stay alive,” but now scientists say even their best-case scenario still shoots past that signature temperature mark.

The U.N. resolution had initially included stronger language from the International Court of Justice opinion that called for establishing an “International Register of Damage” to record evidence and claims, but it was removed after nearly a dozen consultations in order to receive more support.

Advertisement

The adoption came despite reporting by The Associated Press in February that the Trump administration had been urging other nations to press the small island country of Vanuatu — the draft’s original sponsor — to withdraw it from consideration.

In guidance issued to all U.S. embassies and consulates, the State Department had said it “strongly objects” to the proposal and that its adoption “could pose a major threat to U.S. industry.”

On Wednesday, Tammy Bruce, the deputy U.S. ambassador to the U.N., blasted the measure once again, calling it “highly problematic” and maintaining that Washington has serious legal and policy concerns despite changes to the draft.

Advertisement

“The resolution includes inappropriate political demands relating to fossil fuels and on other climate topics,” Bruce told the assembly before the vote.

But representatives from Vanuatu and other island nations, who fear for their survival because of the impact of climate change, said it was important for the General Assembly to back the court opinion, which was hailed as a turning point in international climate law.

“We should be honest with one another about why this matters,” Odo Tevi, the Vanuatu ambassador to the U.N., said before the vote. “It matters because the harm is real and it is already here, along our islands and coastlines, for communities facing drought and failed harvests.”

Advertisement

He added, “The states and peoples bearing the heaviest burden are very often those who contributed least to the problem.”

The action by the world body follows decades of frustration for Pacific nations that are watching their homelands disappear.

In Tuvalu, where the average elevation is just 2 meters (6.6 feet) above sea level, more than a third of the population has applied for a climate migration visa to Australia, although only a limited number are accepted each year. By 2100, much of the country is projected to be underwater at high tide.

Advertisement

In Nauru, the government has begun selling passports to wealthy foreigners — offering visa-free access to dozens of countries — in a bid to generate revenue for possible relocation efforts.

Louis Charbonneau, U.N. director of Human Rights Watch, said in a statement Wednesday that by endorsing the court ruling, the U.N. “reaffirmed the global commitment to protect human rights.” He noted that it came “despite efforts by the U.S. and other oil-producing states to stifle attempts to combat climate change.”

Advertisement

Related

Climate Change

United Nations

António Guterres

Trump's EPA Revokes Scientific Finding That Underpinned U.S. Fight Against Climate Change
Trump Administration Is Dismantling A World-Class Climate, Weather Research Center
Pope Leo Turns Heat Up On Politicians Over Climate At UN Talks Ghosted By Trump Admin
UN Members Push For A Two-State Solution, As More Leaders Recognize Palestinian Statehood

articles.title: Netanyahu Scolds Israeli Security Minister For Releasing Videos Taunting Detained Flotilla Activists
articles.content:
耶路撒冷（美联社）——以色列国家安全部长伊塔马尔·本-格维尔周三发布了嘲笑被拘留船队活动人士的视频，引发总理本雅明·内塔尼亚胡的强烈谴责，并在国际社会引发强烈反响。这些活动人士试图突破以色列对加沙的封锁。

内塔尼亚胡表示，尽管以色列有权阻止“哈马斯恐怖分子支持者挑衅性的船队”，但国家安全部长本-格维尔对待这些活动人士的方式“不符合以色列的价值观和规范”。

本-格维尔周三发布的视频显示他在约430名被拘留者中走动。其中一段视频中，活动人士双手被绑在背后跪下，头部贴着地面，地点似乎是临时拘留区或一艘船的甲板上。

“欢迎来到以色列，我们是这里的主人，”本-格维尔挥舞着大型以色列国旗说道。一名戴着手铐的活动人士高喊“解放巴勒斯坦”，随即被安保人员推倒在地。

在另一段视频中，本-格维尔称这些活动人士“来这里时趾高气扬，像大英雄一样。现在看看他们，”同时呼吁内塔尼亚胡允许他关押这些人。

以色列领导人呼吁快速驱逐活动人士

“我对内塔尼亚胡总理说，把他们交给我很长时间，很长很长时间，把他们交给我们关进恐怖分子监狱，”本-格维尔说。

内塔尼亚胡表示，他已下令“尽快”驱逐这些活动人士。

本-格维尔激怒了以色列外交部长吉迪恩·萨阿尔，萨阿尔在社交媒体X上公开批评这位同僚，称“你在这种可耻的表演中故意损害了我们的国家。”

“不，你不是以色列的面孔，”萨阿尔写道。

本-格维尔在以色列议会回击萨阿尔，指责他“向恐怖分子弯腰”，并表示任何向活动人士道歉都会传递出“软弱”、“屈服”和“投降”的信息。

以色列被指控羞辱活动人士

总部位于以色列的法律倡导组织——阿拉伯少数民族权利法律中心（Adalah）指控以色列当局“对活动人士采取犯罪性质的虐待和羞辱政策”。

Adalah的声明称，这与以色列当局此前在船队行动中对待活动人士的类似虐待模式一致，“以色列对此毫无问责”。

Adalah律师苏哈德·比沙拉告诉美联社，一个由11名律师组成的团体在探视被拘留者后得知，至少有两名活动人士在被橡胶子弹击中后住院治疗，“没有任何原因，没有任何理由。”比沙拉表示，活动人士将于周四出庭，法官将决定驱逐时间。

船队发言人拉尼亚·巴特里斯表示，本-格维尔发布此类视频是因为世界没有追究以色列的责任。

“如果他们对欧洲人、美国人和南非人以及世界各地的人都这样做，想象一下他们对巴勒斯坦人民做了什么，”巴特里斯在网上接受美联社采访时说。

巴特里斯敦促各国政府加大回应力度。“措辞强硬的信件不是我们现在需要的。我们需要更多行动。”

英国外交大臣伊薇特·库珀表示，这段视频“违反了最基本的尊重和尊严标准”，并要求以色列当局作出解释。

意大利谴责被拘留活动人士遭受的待遇是侵犯人类尊严的行为，称本-格维尔的视频“令人无法接受”。意大利还召见以色列驻罗马大使提出抗议，要求立即释放意大利被拘留者。加拿大外交部长阿尼塔·阿南德表示，她已指示官员召见以色列驻渥太华大使。

土耳其和希腊都谴责以色列对待活动人士的方式。土耳其外交部表示，这一行为“向世界公然展示了以色列政府的暴力和野蛮心态”。希腊外交部称本-格维尔的行动“令人无法接受，完全应该受到谴责”，并表示已提出正式抗议。

巴勒斯坦激进组织哈马斯指责本-格维尔对活动人士进行“虐待和羞辱的场景”，称其展示了以色列的“道德堕落和虐待狂”。

以色列拦截所有剩余船队船只

以色列军队周二登上最后一批试图挑战封锁的船队船只——这是突出加沙近200万人严峻条件的最新努力。

船队组织者声称，以色列士兵在拦截过程中向五艘船开火，造成一些损坏。以色列外交部表示，没有使用实弹，“非致命手段”只是作为警告瞄准船只，未针对或伤害抗议者。

根据船队网站，以色列海军从距加沙海岸约268公里（167英里）处开始阻止这支上周从土耳其出发的船队。

以色列称这支船队“是为哈马斯服务的公关噱头”，并无真正向加沙运送援助的意图。这些船只携带的是象征性的援助物资。

周一，以色列海军在塞浦路斯附近国际水域阻止了船队的41艘船并拘留了船上人员。

船上载有十多名爱尔兰公民，其中包括爱尔兰总统凯瑟琳·康诺利的妹妹。爱尔兰总理米歇尔·马丁称以色列在国际水域拦截船只“完全不可接受”。

然而，美国财政部对船上的多名欧洲活动人士实施制裁，美国财政部长斯科特·贝森特称他们为“支持恐怖主义”。

加沙海岸封锁近二十年

自2007年哈马斯控制加沙地带以来，以色列一直维持对该地区的海上封锁。以色列在哈马斯领导的激进分子于2023年10月7日对以色列南部发动袭击后加强了封锁，那次袭击造成约1200人死亡，250多人被扣为人质。

批评者称封锁等同于集体惩罚。以色列表示，封锁旨在防止哈马斯武装自己。埃及是唯一不受以色列控制的加沙边境过境点，该国也大幅限制了进出加沙的人员流动。

以色列在10月7日袭击后发动的报复性军事行动已造成超过72700人死亡，加沙卫生部表示。该卫生部是哈马斯领导的加沙政府的一部分，不区分平民和战斗人员。该部门由医疗专业人员组成，维护和发布详细记录，国际社会普遍认为这些记录可靠。

——

哈吉科斯蒂斯于塞浦路斯尼科西亚报道。美联社记者阿里耶·哈兹布恩、艾萨克·沙尔夫和易卜拉欣·哈兹布恩（耶路撒冷）、萨米·马格迪（开罗）、贾达·赞帕诺（罗马）、安德鲁·威尔克斯（安卡拉，土耳其）和德里克·加托普洛斯（雅典，希腊）亦对此报道有贡献。
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

JERUSALEM (AP) — Israel’s national security minister drew a sharp rebuke from Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and triggered a backlash abroad Wednesday, after releasing videos taunting detained flotilla activists who tried to breach Israel’s blockade of Gaza, telling them they should be imprisoned for a long time.

Netanyahu said that although Israel has every right to stop “provocative flotillas of Hamas terrorist supporters,” the way National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir dealt with the activists was “not in line with Israel’s values and norms.”

Advertisement

Ben-Gvir released videos Wednesday showing him walking among some of the approximately 430 detainees. In one, activists with their hands tied behind their backs are kneeling, their heads touching the floor inside what appears to be a makeshift detention area and on the deck of a ship.

“Welcome to Israel, we are the landlords,” says Ben-Gvir, waving a large Israeli flag. One handcuffed activist shouts “Free Palestine” as Ben-Gvir walks by and is immediately pushed to the ground by security personnel.

In another video, Ben-Gvir says the activists “came here all full of pride like big heroes. Look at them now,” while appealing to Netanyahu to grant him permission to imprison them.

Advertisement

Israel’s Leader Calls For Quick Deportation Of Activists

“I say to Prime Minister Netanyahu, give them to me for a long, long time, give them to us for the terrorist prisons,” Ben-Gvir said.

Netanyahu said he’s given instructions that the activists be deported “as soon as possible.”

Ben-Gvir drew the ire of Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar, who publicly chastised his fellow minister on X, saying “you knowingly caused harm to our State in this disgraceful display.”

Advertisement

“No, you are not the face of Israel,” Saar wrote.

Ben-Gvir shot back at Saar in the Israeli parliament, accusing him of “bowing to the terrorists” and that any Israeli apology to the activists would send a message of “weakness,” “submission” and “surrender.”

Israel Accused Of Humiliating Activists

An Israel-based legal advocacy group, the Legal Center for Arab Minority Rights in Israel, or Adalah, accused Israeli authorities of “employing a criminal policy of abuse and humiliation against activists.”

Advertisement

Adalah’s statement said this followed similar patterns of ill-treatment by Israeli authorities against activists in previous flotilla missions “for which Israel faced zero accountability.”

Adalah lawyer Suhad Bishara told The Associated Press a group of 11 lawyers who visited the detainees is aware of at least two activists who were hospitalized after being shot with rubber bullets “for no reason, without any justification.” Bishara said the activists will be brought before a judge Thursday who will decide on when their deportation will begin.

Flotilla spokesperson Rania Batrice said Ben-Gvir posts such videos because the world hasn’t held Israel to account.

Advertisement

“If they’re doing that to Europeans and Americans and people from South Africa and all over the world, imagine what they’re doing to the Palestinian people,” Batrice told the AP in an online interview.

Batrice urged governments to step up their response. “Strongly worded letters are not what we need right now. We need more action.”

British Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper said the video “violates the most basic standards of respect and dignity” in how people should be treated and demanded an explanation from Israeli authorities.

Advertisement

Italy condemned the detained activists’ treatment as a violation of human dignity and called Ben-Gvir’s videos “unacceptable.” It also summoned Israel’s ambassador in Rome to protest the treatment of Italian detainees and demand their immediate release. Canadian Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand said she’s directed her officials to summon the Israeli ambassador to Ottawa.

Both Turkey and Greece condemned Israel’s treatment of the activists. The Turkish Foreign Ministry said the behavior “openly demonstrated to the world the violent and barbaric mindset” of Israel’s government. The Greek Foreign Ministry called Ben-Gvir’s actions “unacceptable and entirely condemnable” and said it had lodged a formal protest.

Advertisement

Palestinian militant group Hamas called out Ben-Gvir for the “scenes of abuse and humiliation” of the activists, saying they show Israel’s “moral decadence and sadism.”

Israel Intercepts All Remaining Flotilla Boats

Israeli forces on Tuesday boarded the last of the flotilla boats that tried to challenge the blockade — the latest effort to highlight the grim conditions for nearly 2 million Palestinians in Gaza.

Advertisement

Flotilla organizers claimed Israeli soldiers fired on five boats during the interdictions, causing some damage. Israel’s Foreign Ministry said no live ammunition was fired and that “nonlethal means” were aimed at the vessels as a warning, but without targeting or injuring protesters.

Israeli forces had begun stopping the flotilla, which had departed last week from Turkey, around 268 kilometers (167 miles) from the Gaza coastline, according to the flotilla’s website.

Israel has called the flotilla “a PR stunt at the service of Hamas” with no real intent to deliver aid to Gaza. The boats carry a symbolic amount of aid.

Advertisement

On Monday, the Israeli navy stopped 41 boats from the flotilla in international waters off Cyprus and detained those on board.

More than a dozen Irish nationals were aboard the flotilla, including Irish President Catherine Connolly’s sister. Irish Prime Minister Micheál Martin has called Israel’s interception of the boats in international waters “absolutely unacceptable.”

The U.S. Treasury, however, imposed sanctions against several European activists aboard the flotilla, which U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent called “pro-terror.”

Advertisement

Gaza’s Coast Blockaded For Nearly Two Decades

Israel has maintained a sea blockade of Gaza since Hamas took control of the territory in 2007. Israeli authorities intensified it after the Hamas-led militant attacks on southern Israel that killed around 1,200 people and saw more than 250 taken hostage on Oct. 7, 2023.

Critics say the blockade amounts to collective punishment. Israel says it’s intended to prevent Hamas from arming itself. Egypt, which has the only border crossing with Gaza not controlled by Israel, has also greatly restricted movement in and out.

Israel’s retaliatory offensive following the Oct. 7 attacks that started the war has killed more than 72,700 people, according to Gaza’s Health Ministry. The ministry, part of Gaza’s Hamas-run government, doesn’t give a breakdown between civilians and combatants. It is staffed by medical professionals who maintain and publish detailed records viewed as generally reliable by the international community.

Advertisement

___

Hadjicostis reported from Nicosia, Cyprus. Associated Press journalists Areej Hazboun, Isaac Scharf and Ibrahim Hazboun in Jerusalem; Samy Magdy in Cairo; Giada Zampano in Rome; Andrew Wilks in Ankara, Turkey, and Derek Gatopoulos in Athens, Greece, contributed to this report.

Related

Israel

gaza

Turkey

Benjamin Netanyahu

Itamar Ben Gvir

Israel Draws Outrage For Seizing Gaza-Bound Aid Boat And Detaining Activists
Israeli Forces Accused Of Threatening And Abusing Detained Flotilla Activists
'Freedom Flotilla' Tries Again To Break Israel's Blockade On Gaza
